risks: Concept-collision risk: StudyQuest already does upload -> AI questions -> 'Boss Battle' mode. Differentiation MUST be executed and made visible (source-grounding/citations + a true roguelike campaign mapped to syllabus structure + teacher review/monitor), or the project reads as a StudyQuest clone. Name the gap explicitly in the README.

Commodity-feature trap: 'generate questions from an upload' is now table stakes (Quizizz, Quizgecko, Conker, MagicSchool). If the portfolio emphasizes generation rather than grounding + evals + guardrails, it will not stand out to an AI-engineer reviewer.

Scope creep: a 'full' roguelike (procedural maps, meta-progression, dozens of relics/bosses) plus a full LMS is far too much for a solo, free-tier, no-GPU portfolio build. Constrain to one campaign shape, 2-3 question types, 2-3 boss mechanics; cache the generated game spec so OpenAI spend is one-time-per-upload.

Grounding is genuinely hard: PDFs/slides/DOCX vary wildly (scanned images, tables, multi-column). Ingestion + reliable span-level provenance is the riskiest engineering piece — but it is also the differentiator, so budget time accordingly and have a fallback (page-level rather than span-level citation).

Game-vs-learning tension (the Blooket/Gimkit failure mode): if RNG/relics/HP dominate, students win by luck not knowledge. Ensure mechanics reward correctness (streak multipliers, damage = mastery) and that wrong answers always have a learning consequence/feedback loop.

Compliance optics: real classroom use implies FERPA/COPPA and student PII. For a public portfolio demo, avoid storing real minors' PII (use synthetic rosters/class codes) and state the compliance posture, mirroring MagicSchool's SOC2/FERPA/COPPA signaling — otherwise the LMS claim looks naive.

Source freshness: several supporting stats (retention %, 'hours saved', user counts) come from vendor blogs/listicles and should be treated as directional, not authoritative; official pages (Khanmigo, Quizizz, StudyQuest) were prioritized for hard product facts but model/price details still change frequently in 2026.

summary: For SyllabusSlayer's roguelike quiz-RPG, the right schema is a flat-ish, reference-based model that fits OpenAI Structured Outputs' hard constraints (max 100 object properties, max 5 nesting levels, 500 enum values, 15K chars across names/defs/enums) rather than a deeply nested tree. The biggest design risk is that Structured Outputs uses ONE schema per call with strict mode (every field required, additionalProperties:false, root must be an object, no pattern/format/min/max enforcement), so a single monolithic Campaign→Act→Encounter→Question schema would blow the depth/property budget and produce poor generation. The recommended approach is a discriminated-union Question model (a "questionType" enum tag plus a single flat union of per-type fields), generated in stages: first an outline pass (campaign + acts + encounter stubs), then per-encounter question batches. Combat/RPG tuning lives in a separate, deterministic layer the backend computes — NOT something the LLM invents — so balance stays sane and the LLM only fills pedagogical fields. Source-grounding (chunk IDs + quote) is a required field on every question to support the "serious AI engineering" eval/guardrail story. Existing standards (QTI 3.0 interactions, H5P Question Set/Branching Scenario, Kahoot's flat question/choices/points/time model, GIFT) inform the question taxonomy but are all too heavy or too text-based to adopt wholesale; the proposed schema borrows their question-type vocabulary while staying lean enough for reliable LLM generation and clean Pydantic/Zod mirroring.

options.name: Discriminated-union Question model + staged generation (RECOMMENDED)
options.whatItIs: One Question object carries a 'questionType' enum tag and a flat set of optional-by-null per-type fields (options[], correctOptionIds[], pairs[], orderedItems[], acceptedAnswers[], etc.). Campaign/Act/Encounter/Question hierarchy exists logically but is generated in separate LLM calls (outline pass, then per-encounter question batches) and stitched by ID references, never as one giant nested payload.
options.pros: Each LLM call stays well under the 100-property/5-level Structured Outputs limits; strict-mode-friendly (all fields required, nulls for inapplicable type fields); maps 1:1 to a Pydantic discriminated union and a Zod discriminatedUnion; staged generation keeps each call cheap on gpt-5.4-mini and enables per-stage evals/retries; flat union is the pattern OpenAI itself recommends over deep nesting.
options.cons: More orchestration code (multiple calls, ID stitching, assembly/validation layer); the 'all type-fields present but null' shape is slightly verbose; requires a post-validation step to assert per-type field presence (Structured Outputs won't enforce 'if MCQ then options non-empty').
options.fitForProject: Best fit. Directly satisfies (a) reliable Structured Outputs generation, (b) full client renderability via a discriminator switch in React, (c) dual validation. Staged calls suit the portfolio budget and showcase real AI-engineering pipeline design.

options.name: Single monolithic nested schema (Campaign with embedded acts→encounters→questions)
options.whatItIs: One Pydantic/JSON schema describing the entire campaign tree, passed in a single Structured Outputs call that returns the whole game at once.
options.pros: Conceptually simplest; one call, one object, one validation; no ID-stitching.
options.cons: Campaign→Act→Encounter→Question→options[].field is already ~5+ levels deep, hitting the nesting cap; total property count across all nested objects easily exceeds 100; large single generations are slower, costlier, more error-prone, and harder to retry/eval granularly; a single malformed deep branch fails the entire game.
options.fitForProject: Poor fit. Violates the explicit 'avoid excessive nesting, prefer flat referenced structures' requirement and the measured 5-level/100-property limits.

options.name: Adopt an existing standard wholesale (QTI 3.0 or H5P content.json)
options.whatItIs: Emit QTI 3.0 XML/JSON interactions (qti-choice-interaction, qti-match-interaction, qti-order-interaction) or H5P content.json (Question Set / Branching Scenario) directly from the LLM.
options.pros: Battle-tested pedagogical taxonomy and accessibility metadata; interoperable with real LMSs; signals domain awareness.
options.cons: QTI is verbose XML-derived with response-processing templates and shared-vocabulary CSS classes — far too heavy to generate reliably via Structured Outputs and overkill to render in a custom Next.js game; H5P content.json is library-versioned, deeply nested, and built for its own player runtime, not a roguelike combat UI; neither models HP/damage/relics.
options.fitForProject: Use as inspiration only. Borrow the interaction-type vocabulary (choice/multi/order/match/gap-match) and Bloom tagging, but do not adopt the wire formats — they fight Structured Outputs limits and add no game-combat value.

options.name: Flat Kahoot/GIFT-style quiz model (no RPG layer)
options.whatItIs: A flat list of questions with text/choices/correct/points/time, like Kahoot's JSON or Moodle GIFT, with combat bolted on as ad-hoc fields.
options.pros: Extremely simple and very reliable to generate; trivially renderable; matches familiar quiz tools.
options.cons: No hierarchy mapping to syllabus topics, no encounter/boss structure, no relics/XP/leveling, no source-grounding, no Bloom difficulty; would make SyllabusSlayer just a quiz, not a roguelike — undercutting the core product pitch and the AI-engineering depth.
options.fitForProject: Insufficient alone, but its flatness is the right instinct. The recommended schema keeps the flat-question virtue while adding the campaign hierarchy and a separate deterministic combat layer.

recommendation:
RECOMMENDED ARCHITECTURE: Split the data into a PEDAGOGICAL layer (LLM-generated via Structured Outputs) and a COMBAT/RPG TUNING layer (deterministically computed by the FastAPI backend from difficulty/Bloom + designer constants). The LLM should NEVER invent HP/damage numbers — it only fills educational fields plus a per-item difficulty/Bloom tag and source grounding. Generate in two staged calls to stay under Structured Outputs limits (max 100 properties, 5 nesting levels, 500 enums, 15K chars of names/defs/enums; strict mode = every field required + additionalProperties:false + root is an object; pattern/format/min/max are NOT enforced so validate ranges in code; optional fields are expressed as type unions with null).

STAGE 1 (Outline): one Structured Outputs call -> Campaign + Acts + Encounter STUBS (no questions). STAGE 2 (per encounter): one call per encounter -> a batch of Questions. Backend stitches by IDs and computes combat tuning. This keeps every individual schema shallow (<=3 levels) and small.

------------------------------------------------------------
PYDANTIC-STYLE SCHEMA (backend source of truth; mirror in Zod on frontend)
------------------------------------------------------------

# ---- ENUMS ----
QuestionType = Enum: "multiple_choice" | "multi_select" | "true_false" | "short_answer" | "ordering" | "matching"
BloomLevel   = Enum: "remember" | "understand" | "apply" | "analyze" | "evaluate" | "create"   # revised Bloom (Anderson/Krathwohl 2001)
Difficulty   = Enum: "easy" | "medium" | "hard"
EncounterKind= Enum: "minion" | "elite" | "boss"
RewardKind   = Enum: "relic" | "powerup" | "xp" | "heal"

# ---- STAGE 1 OUTPUT: CampaignOutline (one Structured Outputs call) ----
CampaignOutline:
  schemaVersion: str                      # e.g. "1.0.0" (validate in code)
  campaignId: str                         # backend may overwrite with a UUID
  title: str
  description: str
  sourceDocumentId: str                   # which uploaded resource this came from
  acts: list[ActStub]                     # array of objects (flat, not deeply nested)

ActStub:                                  # one Act == one top-level syllabus topic
  actId: str
  order: int
  title: str
  syllabusTopic: str                      # the mapped topic/chapter heading from the source
  summary: str
  encounters: list[EncounterStub]

EncounterStub:                            # boss/elite/minion fight, no questions yet
  encounterId: str
  order: int
  kind: EncounterKind
  title: str                              # e.g. "Mitochondria, the Powerhouse Boss"
  enemyName: str
  enemyFlavor: str                        # short narrative blurb (Twine/Ink-style flavor)
  subTopic: str                           # sub-section of the act this fight covers
  targetQuestionCount: int                # how many questions Stage 2 should generate
  # NOTE: depth here = CampaignOutline.acts[].encounters[] = 3 levels. Safe.

# ---- STAGE 2 OUTPUT: QuestionBatch (one call per encounter) ----
QuestionBatch:
  encounterId: str                        # echoes the stub it belongs to
  questions: list[Question]

# ---- THE DISCRIMINATED-UNION QUESTION (the heart of the schema) ----
# Single flat object. questionType is the discriminator. Per-type fields are
# present but null when not applicable (strict-mode requires all fields present).
Question:
  questionId: str
  questionType: QuestionType              # discriminator
  bloomLevel: BloomLevel
  difficulty: Difficulty
  prompt: str                             # the question stem / rich text
  # --- source grounding (REQUIRED on every item; powers evals + guardrails) ---
  sourceChunkIds: list[str]               # ids of the retrieved chunks used
  sourceQuote: str                        # verbatim snippet the item is grounded in
  sourcePage: int | null                  # page/slide number if known
  # --- feedback ---
  explanation: str                        # why the correct answer is correct
  hint: str | null                        # optional pre-answer nudge
  # --- per-type fields (exactly one cluster is non-null per questionType) ---
  options: list[Option] | null            # multiple_choice, multi_select
  correctOptionIds: list[str] | null      # ids into options[] (1 for MC, >=1 for multi)
  correctBoolean: bool | null             # true_false
  acceptedAnswers: list[str] | null       # short_answer (case-insensitive accept list)
  caseSensitive: bool | null              # short_answer
  orderedItems: list[OrderedItem] | null  # ordering (correct sequence by .order)
  matchPairs: list[MatchPair] | null      # matching

Option:
  optionId: str
  text: str

OrderedItem:
  itemId: str
  text: str
  order: int                              # the CORRECT position (1-based)

MatchPair:
  pairId: str
  left: str
  right: str                              # correct counterpart of left

# Max nesting in Stage 2: QuestionBatch.questions[].options[].text = 3 levels. Safe.
# Property count per call is well under 100. Enum values total < 30. Good.

------------------------------------------------------------
COMBAT / RPG TUNING (computed by backend, NOT by the LLM)
------------------------------------------------------------
Stored alongside the generated content (in DB / served to client), produced
deterministically from difficulty + Bloom + designer constants so balance is stable:

CombatConfig (per campaign):
  playerStartingHp: int                   # e.g. 100
  baseDamagePerCorrect: int               # e.g. 10
  streakMultipliers: list[float]          # e.g. [1.0, 1.25, 1.5, 2.0] indexed by streak tier
  wrongAnswerHpCost: int                  # e.g. 8
  xpPerCorrectByDifficulty: {easy:int, medium:int, hard:int}  # e.g. {10,20,35}
  levelXpCurve: list[int]                 # cumulative XP thresholds per level

EncounterCombat (per encounter, derived):
  enemyMaxHp: int                         # = sum over questions of expectedDamage, scaled by kind
  enemyBaseDamage: int                    # damage enemy deals on a wrong/timeout
  kindHpMultiplier: float                 # minion 1.0 / elite 1.5 / boss 2.5

Reward (per encounter or level-up):
  rewardId: str
  kind: RewardKind                        # relic | powerup | xp | heal
  name: str
  description: str
  magnitude: int                          # e.g. +15 max HP, +0.25 streak mult, etc.
# Relic/powerup CATALOG is authored once by you (a fixed table); the backend
# assigns drops. The LLM may optionally THEME a relic name to the topic, but
# never sets magnitudes.

------------------------------------------------------------

------------------------------------------------------------
IMPLEMENTATION NOTES FOR THIS PROJECT
------------------------------------------------------------
- Pydantic: model Question as a single class with all fields, OR use a typed
  Union[MultipleChoiceQ, MultiSelectQ, ...] with Field(discriminator="questionType").
  The single-flat-class-with-nulls form is the safest for Structured Outputs strict
  mode; convert to the rich discriminated union in a post-parse step where you also
  run a validator asserting the right cluster is non-null per type and ranges are sane
  (Structured Outputs does NOT enforce min/max/non-empty).
- Frontend: Zod z.discriminatedUnion("questionType", [...]) gives you exhaustive
  switch rendering and compile-time safety in the React renderer.
- Model choice: use gpt-5.4-mini ($0.75 in / $4.50 out per 1M) for question batches
  with Structured Outputs; reserve gpt-5.4 ($2.50/$15) for the outline/quality-critical
  passes. Cache the system prompt (90% cached-input discount) since it repeats per call.
- Keep schemaVersion in every payload so stored games survive schema evolution.
keyFacts: OpenAI Structured Outputs limits (2026): max 100 object properties total, max 5 levels of nesting, max 500 enum values total, and combined names/definitions/enum/const characters must not exceed 15,000.

Structured Outputs strict mode requires: every property listed in 'required' (no native optionals), additionalProperties:false on every object, and the root must be an object (cannot return a bare array — wrap as {items:[...]}).
Optional fields in strict mode are expressed as a type union with null, e.g. type:['string','null']; the field still must be marked required.
Structured Outputs does NOT enforce pattern, format, minLength, minimum, maximum — content/range validation must be done in application code.
OpenAI's CFG-based Structured Outputs DOES support recursive schemas via $ref:'#', but flatter array/enum-based schemas are still recommended for reliability.
First request with a new schema incurs extra latency while OpenAI compiles+caches it; subsequent calls with the same schema are fast.
Revised Bloom's taxonomy (Anderson & Krathwohl, 2001) has six verb-based levels: remember, understand, apply, analyze, evaluate, create (Evaluate now below Create).

QTI 3.0 interaction types include qti-choice-interaction, qti-match-interaction, qti-order-interaction, qti-associate-interaction, qti-gap-match-interaction, extended-text — the vocabulary maps cleanly to MC/multi/matching/ordering/short-answer.

H5P container types Question Set and Branching Scenario bundle question types (Single Choice Set, Multichoice, True/False, Drag the Text, Mark the Words, Image Sequencing) and store config as JSON in content.json inside a versioned .h5p package.

Kahoot's quiz JSON is flat: a questions array where each question has question text, time (ms), points, numberOfAnswers, and a choices array of {answer, correct}; single-select up to 1000 pts, multi-select up to 500 pts/correct, 2-6 answers.
GIFT (Moodle) is a plain-text picoformat (=correct, ~distractor, T/F, {} answer blocks) for MC, true/false, short-answer, matching, missing-word, numerical, essay — not JSON.

OpenAI API pricing (June 2026, per 1M tokens, standard tier): gpt-5.5 $5.00 in / $0.50 cached / $30.00 out; gpt-5.4 $2.50 / $0.25 / $15.00; gpt-5.4-mini $0.75 / $0.075 / $4.50; gpt-5.4-nano $0.20 / $0.02 / $1.25.
gpt-5.5 supports Structured Outputs; Structured Outputs carries no separate fee (tokens billed at the model's normal rates); cached input gets ~90% discount which helps repeated system prompts.

risks: Structured Outputs will NOT enforce that the correct per-type field cluster is populated (e.g. a multiple_choice item could come back with null options) or that ranges/non-empty hold — you MUST add a backend post-validation pass (Pydantic validators) and a frontend Zod refine; otherwise the client renderer can crash on malformed items.

The 'all type-fields present, null when unused' pattern bloats token output (every question carries 8 mostly-null fields). For very large campaigns this raises cost and can approach the 100-property limit if you later add many types; mitigate by generating per-encounter batches and keeping the question union lean.

Letting the LLM set combat numbers (HP/damage/XP) would make balance unpredictable and exploitable; the design deliberately computes these deterministically in the backend — if a future contributor moves tuning into the LLM schema, game balance and difficulty will regress.

Source-grounding fields (sourceQuote/sourceChunkIds) are only as trustworthy as your retrieval+prompt; the LLM can fabricate quotes. Add a guardrail that verifies sourceQuote is a substring (fuzzy) of the referenced chunk, and flag/reject items that fail — this is also a strong eval to showcase.

short_answer auto-grading via acceptedAnswers[] is brittle for free-text; either restrict short_answer to fill-in-the-blank with tightly-bounded answers, or add an LLM-judge grading path (extra cost) — document the tradeoff to avoid frustrating wrong-answer penalties in combat.

Deep model/pricing volatility: model names (gpt-5.4-mini) and prices verified for June 2026 but change frequently (gpt-5.5 doubled prices on 2026-04-23); re-verify against the OpenAI pricing page before committing budget, and keep the model id in config, not hardcoded.
schemaVersion is included but must actually be enforced with a migration strategy; without it, evolving the question taxonomy will break previously generated/stored games in the LMS dashboard.

ordering/matching are harder for the client to render and grade than MC/TF; if demo time is tight, ship MC + multi_select + true_false first (highest generation reliability) and gate ordering/matching behind validation maturity.
